cards.front: كِتَاب means ___ and comes from root ___
cards.grammarNote: كِتَاب (kitāb) follows the CiCāC pattern — a common shape for abstract or object nouns. The three consonants ك، ت، ب are the skeleton; the vowels i and ā give it its noun shape.

cards.front: What does the pattern CāCiC (فَاعِل) mean?
cards.back: Active participle — the doer of the action. e.g., كَاتِب (kātib) = writer, عَامِل (ʿāmil) = worker, دَارِس (dāris) = student/studier
cards.grammarNote: The CāCiC pattern is one of the most productive in Arabic. Spot it by the long ā vowel after the first root consonant and a kasra (i) under the second. It always means 'the one who does X.'

cards.back: Root د-ر-س (d-r-s) = studying/learning. مَدْرَسَة (madrasa) = school — a place of studying.
cards.grammarNote: مَدْرَسَة follows the maCCaCa pattern — a place-noun template. The م prefix + root + short a vowels = 'the place where this root's action happens.' Same pattern as مَكْتَبَة (maktaba, library/bookstore).

cards.back: مَكْتُوب (maktūb) = written / a letter — something that has been written.
cards.grammarNote: The maCCūC (مَفْعُول) pattern is the passive participle across virtually all roots. The long ū vowel on the third consonant is the key marker. Compare: مَكْتَب (maktab, office — place noun, short a) vs. مَكْتُوب (maktūb, written — passive, long ū).

cards.front: What root do كَتَبَ، كِتَاب، كَاتِب، and مَكْتَبَة all share?
cards.back: Root ك-ت-ب (k-t-b) = writing. All four words contain the consonants ك، ت، ب in that order.
cards.grammarNote: To find the root, strip away the vowels and any م prefix. What consonants remain, in order? That is your root. كِتَاب → ك ت ب. مَكْتَبَة → (drop م) → ك ت ب.

cards.front: What role does the maCCaC / maCCaCa pattern play?
cards.back: Place of action noun. مَكْتَب (maktab) = office/desk, مَدْرَسَة (madrasa) = school, مَعْمَل (maʿmal) = laboratory.
cards.grammarNote: Any word starting with م followed by a root's consonants with short a vowels is likely a place noun. The feminine ending ـة often appears on place nouns too: مَكْتَبَة (library), مَدْرَسَة (school).

cards.back: دَرَسَ (darasa) = he studied. Pattern: CaCaCa (فَعَلَ) — the standard Form I past tense verb pattern.
cards.grammarNote: The CaCaCa pattern (three short a vowels across three root consonants) is the most common Form I past tense shape. كَتَبَ (kataba, he wrote), دَرَسَ (darasa, he studied), عَمِلَ (ʿamila, he worked — slight variant with i on the second vowel).

cards.front: عَامِل (ʿāmil) means ___ and shares its pattern with ___
cards.back: عَامِل (ʿāmil) = worker/factor. CāCiC pattern — same as كَاتِب (kātib, writer) and دَارِس (dāris, studier).
cards.grammarNote: Root ع-م-ل (ʿ-m-l) = work/doing. عَامِل is the active participle: long ā after ع, kasra under م. The CāCiC pattern reliably produces the 'doer' meaning across all roots.

cards.front: مُدَرِّس (mudarris) means ___ — why does it look different from دَارِس?
cards.back: مُدَرِّس (mudarris) = teacher. The doubled middle consonant (رّ) signals Form II — an intensive/causative form. A mudarris is one who causes others to study.
cards.grammarNote: Form II verbs double the middle root consonant and use the prefix مُـ in their active participle. مُدَرِّس (teacher) vs. دَارِس (studier/student). Form II often means 'to cause X' or 'to do X intensively,' so the doer is frequently a teacher, trainer, or professional.

cards.front: كُتُب (kutub) is the plural of كِتَاب. What type of plural is this?
cards.back: Broken plural (جمع التكسير, jamʿ al-taksīr) — the vowel pattern changes rather than adding a suffix. كِتَاب → كُتُب.
cards.grammarNote: Arabic often forms plurals by changing internal vowels rather than adding endings — these are called broken plurals. There are many patterns; CuCuC (كُتُب, كُتُب) is one common one. Broken plurals must often be memorised per word, but seeing the same root consonants tells you what the plural means.

cards.front: مَعْمَل (maʿmal) comes from root ___ and means ___
cards.back: Root ع-م-ل (ʿ-m-l) = work/doing. مَعْمَل (maʿmal) = laboratory / factory — the place where work is done.
cards.grammarNote: maCCaC place-noun pattern at work again: م + ع-م-ل + short a vowels = a place of work. Same template as مَكْتَب (office, from writing root) and the implied place-nouns from dozens of other roots.

cards.front: You see an unfamiliar Arabic word. What is the first thing to look for?
cards.back: The 3-consonant root skeleton. Strip vowels and affixes (especially a leading م). Identify the three consonants in order — they point to the core meaning.
cards.grammarNote: Arabic has hundreds of roots. Once you know a root, you can often guess 8–10 related words. The patterns — CaCaCa (past verb), CāCiC (doer), maCCaC (place), maCCūC (passive), CiCāC (noun) — repeat across every root. Learn the patterns once; apply them everywhere.
